ranking.name: The Register — a deck that audits itself
ranking.tagline: Every number in their deck is a clickable claim with its evidence attached, the deck opens with what they DON'T claim, and it publicly commits to which claims will flip from modelled to measured by which date — you're invited back on 30 September to diff it.

verdicts.strengths: This is the rare novelty concept where the innovation is diligence-shaped rather than decorative. As a catalytic funder my actual bottleneck is verification cost: are the traction numbers real, are the quotes consented, is the unit-cost model honest, what happens if the cost-down fails. The concept answers each of those at the moment I ask it — click 496 beds and see the live register plus today's drift-check, click a quote and see the named consent record, click $421 and see which lines are invoice-verified versus modelled grey. For a funder operating in the Indigenous space, consent-per-quote visibility alone collapses a safeguarding diligence item that normally takes weeks of back-and-forth. The credible-commitment devices are the strongest part: the anti-claims cold open pre-empts exactly the over-claiming I screen for (scabies-to-RHD as motivation not outcome is the correct call and most applicants get it wrong); the pre-registered 50-bed experiment with pass/fail thresholds and a publish-either-way pledge converts the recoverable grant's core risk (unproven cost-down) into a fundable, dated experiment — which is precisely what catalytic capital exists to buy; the changelog makes retraction permanently visible, which is a structural honesty bond, not a slide. Crucially, the design degrades gracefully: it keeps the known 14-slide spine and chips are opt-in, so a skimming reader gets a classic excellent deck and loses nothing — downside bounded, upside real. The 'come back 30 September and diff this' lands after my 31 Aug deadline, so it does not invite me to wait; it schedules my post-commitment accountability for me. And I verified the enabling infrastructure exists in the codebase, so the moat claim ('unfakeable because it is wired to real systems') holds. Net effect on my decision: it would compress my trust-formation phase materially and give me committee-ready evidence of operational discipline — the register IS the reference check.
verdicts.weaknesses: Four real deductions. (1) Committee artefact gap: my decision runs through an investment committee that consumes papers, not gated URLs. There is no static, snapshot-dated PDF export in the build list; someone (me) still has to transcribe the deck into a memo, so part of the 'it does the committee's job' claim is overstated. A signed, dated ledger-snapshot PDF export would fix this and is conspicuously absent. (2) Self-attestation is not audit: the evidence drawers show Goods' own OCR of Goods' own invoices reconciled by Goods' own scripts. It proves they are organised and internally consistent — genuinely valuable — but it does not replace independent verification, and a sophisticated sceptic will note that a curated evidence chain can still be curated. Meanwhile the one number I most need — revenue — is LOCKED pending accountant sign-off. Honest, but if Standard Ledger has not signed off before ~mid-August, that chip becomes a reason to slip past 31 Aug, not commit by it; the deck makes the blocker prominent without resolving it. (3) Live data cuts both ways: the LOI ladder at an honest $0 signed broadcasts the cold-start problem in real time to every funder who opens it, and catalytic funders herd; a stale claim, failed drift-check, or broken drawer on viewing day does disproportionate damage because the entire premise is 'audit us'. Fragility risk is higher than a static deck's. (4) Opportunity cost: 3 weeks of a 2-founder, 0-hire team in July, concurrent with the entity/51% gate, 50-bed run prep, and live LOI hustling. The reuse is real so 3 weeks is plausible, but the sceptical question 'why are the founders building deck software in the final month of the raise' is fair and the concept does not answer it. Minor: the self-auditing framing risks reading as extremely sophisticated packaging, which slightly raises rather than lowers my scrutiny of the underlying fundamentals (which remain: unproven cost-down, $0 signed, 2 people).

verdicts.strengths: DELIVERY: This is the rare concept whose "usesExisting" list is 100% verified real — I checked every file. cleared-voices.ts (default-deny, Alfred Johnson/Norman Frank/Ivy/Dianne Stokes all cleared), loi-pipeline.ts (live GHL stage map, MATCH_TARGET with built-in 'do not present as secured' guardrail), funder-pages.ts + proxy.ts per-slug gating, /deck's LabelKind system (verified/modelled/interest/future already implemented at v2/src/app/deck/page.tsx:34), cost-model-scenarios.json with OCR provenance, asset-canonical.ts + getCanonicalAssetRollup() + three existing drift scripts, products.guards.test.ts as the guard-test precedent, cost-story's shared engine and CostPlayground, /bed/[id]. Roughly 80% of this concept is composition over proven infrastructure; the 7 needsBuilding items are UI + one new data file. 3 weeks with an AI agent is plausible (I'd budget 4); July 10 → Aug 31 leaves margin even at 2x slip. Maintenance burden is genuinely low-frequency: claims flip on discrete events (50-bed run, LOI signings), live numbers self-update from Supabase/GHL, and changelog snapshots can be automated as git commits — the founders' ongoing cost is decision-time, not code-time. GATES: Strongest structural compliance of any design possible. Claim ceiling is enforced by schema (no health-outcome claim category exists — a claim that can't be expressed can't be over-claimed), demand is explicitly interest, QBE meter at honest $0 signed matches loi-pipeline.ts's own warning note, cost-down pre-registered as a falsifiable hypothesis. Canon-numbers-only is enforced by rendering FROM canon and exposing drift rather than hiding it. The LOCKED-claim design actually fixes an existing live bug: /deck/page.tsx:508 currently ships the withheld AU$741,111 in client JS on an ungated route. The changelog-as-retraction-record makes over-claiming self-punishing — honesty by construction, not by policy.
verdicts.weaknesses: FAILURE MODES FOUND (all mitigable, several unstated in the design): (1) Live-dependency embarrassment — the design shows only the happy path where the drift check 'agrees'. If an investor clicks '496 beds' and live Supabase disagrees with canon (new scans, stale canon), or the GHL fetch times out mid-drawer, the deck that promised 'click and it proves itself' fails on camera. Needs cached last-known-good fallbacks and honest mismatch copy designed in from day one. (2) LOI ladder confidentiality — 'their own rung highlighted' must not leak OTHER funders' identities or stages to the viewing funder; the design never says the ladder is aggregate-only. (3) Bed embed PII — /bed/[id] includes recipient-card/recipient-panel components; embedding a specific bed's scan log + map in a funder surface is household-adjacent. 'A bed with no owner identity' needs an explicit stripped embed variant, not the full community-facing page. (4) LOCKED-leak via prose — the schema protects the number, but copy-paste from the existing deck (which literally contains AU$741,111) or careless changelog JSON generation re-leaks it; snapshots must be generated from the rendered ledger only, guarded by a test. (5) The dated-promise trap — 31 Aug flip date on the ask and the 30 Sept diff appointment convert any slip into a publicly documented miss inside the sales artifact itself; the publish-either-way pledge only works if the founders actually keep the changelog honest when it hurts, and a changelog showing zero movement is worse than no changelog. (6) Scope-creep of chips — every sentence tempts registration as a claim; 30+ drawers times N funder instances multiplies QA on a 2-person team. (7) Meta-risk: for a less technical investment committee, the audit machinery can overshadow the bed, the communities, and the voices — the emotional spine must survive the instrumentation. Effort estimate of 3 weeks is mildly optimistic once per-funder QA and fallback states are counted.

verdicts.strengths: The core move — claims as versioned, first-class data objects with promised status-flip dates, a public changelog, and a scheduled 'come back 30 September and diff this deck' appointment — is genuinely something I have not seen. I have seen every adjacent species: open-startup dashboards (Buffer, Baremetrics), live-metrics embeds, Notion data rooms, interactive cost playgrounds, gated funder microsites. Those are all present here as supporting furniture, and alone they'd score a 5. What's new is the combination of (a) the anti-claims cold open, (b) pre-registering the 50-bed run like a clinical trial with pass/fail thresholds and a publish-either-way pledge, (c) the LOCKED claim state that displays an unlock condition instead of a number, and (d) converting due diligence into an appointment Goods scheduled for the investor. The partner sentence a week later writes itself: 'They opened with what they don't claim, every number is a button that shows its receipts, and they booked us to come back in September to diff the deck against reality.' That sentence gets said, and it gets forwarded, because the deck does the investment committee's verification work for them. Crucially it's defensible against copycats and verified against this codebase: the default-deny cleared-voices pattern, the QR asset register at /bed/[id], the OCR'd cost model, and the funder-gating pattern all exist today — the 3-week estimate is wiring, not invention. The consent/claim-ceiling handling is structurally enforced (no schema category for health outcomes), which is the right way to do it. And the changelog-as-permanent-retraction-record is a self-binding honesty mechanism, not decoration.
verdicts.weaknesses: Three real risks. First, fragility cuts both ways: a deck that says 'audit us' has zero tolerance for a stale drift-check, a broken evidence drawer, or a dead /bed embed — one broken receipt does more damage than a whole ordinary deck, so QA burden is higher than the 3-week estimate implies, and it lands on a 2-founder team six weeks before the 31 Aug deadline. Second, density vs. attention: partners give decks minutes, not sessions; fifteen slides where every number invites a click risks the narrative drowning in chrome, and a skeptical reader may ask why a 2-person pre-revenue outfit built a claims-provenance system instead of pressing beds — the deck must land the 'the register IS the moat' argument fast or the machinery reads as over-engineering. Third, the diff promise is a deferred payoff: on first view the changelog is nearly empty, so the most novel feature demonstrates itself only if the investor actually returns on 30 September — and if the 50-bed run slips or misses, the deck has pre-committed to publishing the miss during the raise itself. That's the honest bet the whole concept makes, and founders should walk in knowing it. Minor: 'publicly commits' overstates it — these are gated per-funder instances, not a public page.

concept.name: The Register — a deck that audits itself
concept.tagline: Every number in their deck is a clickable claim with its evidence attached, the deck opens with what they DON'T claim, and it publicly commits to which claims will flip from modelled to measured by which date — you're invited back on 30 September to diff it.
concept.coreInnovation: Claims become first-class, versioned data objects. A new `claims-ledger.ts` (default-deny, like `cleared-voices.ts`) registers every externally-usable claim as {id, statement, status: verified|modelled|interest|future|locked, evidence refs, promised-flip date}. The deck renders each number as a ClaimChip; clicking opens an EvidenceDrawer showing the actual chain — the live Supabase asset count from `getCanonicalAssetRollup()` with today's drift-check result, the OCR'd invoice line behind a cost figure from `cost-model-scenarios.json`, the consent record behind a quote from `cleared-voices.ts` (cleared 2026-06-17, by name). The ledger is dated and diffable: the deck names, in advance, the two events that will move claims — the 50-bed run (flips $421 from modelled to measured) and the LOI ladder (flips $0 signed toward $400K) — and shows a changelog of every claim that has ever changed status. No other deck can do this because no other startup has a QR-traceable asset register, an invoice-OCR cost model with per-number confidence, and a formal consent architecture to wire it to.
concept.experienceFlow: The investor opens their gated instance (`/register/[slug]`, funder-pages pattern) and the first screen is black-on-cream text: "What we do not claim." Three lines — we do not claim prevented disease; demand is interest, not revenue; our cost-down is a hypothesis until beds 1–50 come off our own press. Then the ask, one line. From there it reads like the 14-slide deck they know, except every number wears a small labelled chip. They click "496 beds" and a drawer slides open: live count from the asset register, reconciliation date, drift-check ran today, agrees. They click Alfred Johnson's quote and see its consent record. They click "$421" and see the invoice OCR behind the verified lines and honest grey on the modelled ones, with the CostPlayground sliders right there to stress it. Mid-deck they hit The Experiment: the 50-bed run pre-registered like a trial — hypothesis, method, pass/fail thresholds, and what Goods will publish if it misses. Then the raise assembling in real time: the LOI ladder from the live pipeline, QBE match meter honestly at $0 signed, their own name on the next rung. The close: Norman Frank's quote, then "Come back 30 September and diff this page." A changelog link shows every claim that has ever moved, and when.
architecture: 1. Anti-claims cold open — three things Goods does NOT claim (scabies→RHD is the why, not an outcome; interest ≠ revenue; cost-down unproven) before any logo or photo
2. The claim + ask — one line, AU$400K recoverable grant converting to community ownership, with the ask itself rendered as a ClaimChip (status: future, flip date 31 Aug)
3. The floor tonight — Alfred Johnson (Palm Island) quote whose chip opens his consent record from cleared-voices.ts; media-pack golden-hour photo
4. The bed and the making — assembly.mp4 inline, X-trestle explainer, 'the making belongs On Country' with process/ factory photography
5. Traction — 496/9/16/2,660 as live ClaimChips: drawer shows getCanonicalAssetRollup() vs asset-canonical.ts with today's drift-check verdict
6. Trust the register, not the pitch — embedded live /bed/GB0-156-40 record: scan log, event timeline, map; 'pick any bed, we'll show you its life'
7. What a bed really costs — $685→$421 waterfall where each line item carries its cost-model-scenarios.json confidence flag; verified invoice lines open their OCR source; Idiot Index (8.6× shred)
8. Stress it yourself — embedded CostPlayground (cost-story shared engine): price/volume sliders, BUY/MAKE toggle, link out to /investors?skin=investment

9. The Experiment — 50-bed run pre-registered: hypothesis ($421 ± threshold), method, AU$60–80K budget, pass/fail criteria, and the published-either-way commitment; the claim that flips modelled→measured
10. Risk register as a feature, not an appendix — scored risks with owners and gate dates (entity/51% end-July, SEFA independent-director covenant, founder concentration), each a claim object
11. The round assembling in real time — LOI ladder (target→signed→contract→cash) from loi-pipeline.ts, QBE match meter at honest $0 signed, this funder's rung highlighted
12. Why now — micro-manufacturing, IPP 51% threshold tightening 1 July 2026, HDPE + freight economics

13. Team and governance — 2 founders, 0 hires labelled honest; trigger-gated GM (Oct 2026) and BD (Feb 2027); board path that clears SEFA's covenant; one claim shown LOCKED: revenue withheld pending Standard Ledger sign-off, unlock condition stated
14. The changelog — dated ledger of every claim that has changed status since first publication; proof the machine is real, not decoration
15. Close — Norman Frank (Tennant Creek), then the dated invitation: 'Come back 30 September and diff this deck'; CTA to book the Stage 2 walkthrough
usesExisting: /deck's LabelKind system (verified/modelled/interest/future) and its 14-slide narrative spine — upgraded from static badge to interactive claim object
asset-canonical.ts + getCanonicalAssetRollup() in impact-fetcher.ts + the existing drift-check script (live-vs-canon agreement shown in-drawer)
cost-model-scenarios.ts/.json v6 with invoice-OCR verification and per-number confidence flags
cost-story's shared cost-model engine (cost-model-context.tsx), CostPlayground (play.tsx), and Recharts charts; /investors?skin=investment cockpit as deep-link
/bed/[id] live Supabase-backed bed records (embed GB0-156-40 or similar)
loi-pipeline.ts LOI ladder (LoiRung target/signed/contract/cash) + MATCH_TARGET wired to live GHL pipelines
cleared-voices.ts default-deny allowlist + curated-quotes.ts (Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Dianne Stokes, Norman Frank)
funder-pages.ts per-slug password gating pattern in proxy.ts for personalised instances
media-pack and process/ photography, stretch-bed/assembly.mp4, brand tokens (--goods-terracotta etc., Georgia display)
funder-shared-content.ts atoms (TRACTION_STATS, CAPITAL_STACK, QBE_PROGRAM, INVESTMENT_THESIS)
needsBuilding: claims-ledger.ts — default-deny registry of claim objects {id, statement, status, evidenceRefs, flipCondition, flipDate, history[]}; the single new source of truth
ClaimChip + EvidenceDrawer components (client) rendering evidence chains: canon-vs-live counts, invoice OCR excerpts, consent records, Xero-reconciliation summary cards (redacted, no raw ledger)
/register/[slug] route: the deck itself, reusing /deck's keyboard nav and chrome-free overlay, gated per-funder via proxy.ts pattern
Changelog/diff view: dated JSON snapshots of the ledger committed on each change, rendered as a human-readable 'what moved and why' page
The Experiment page: 50-bed run pre-registration (hypothesis, method, thresholds, publish-either-way pledge) that the measured results later overwrite
LOI ladder deck widget reading loi-pipeline.ts stage data with the viewing funder's rung highlighted
LOCKED claim state rendering (revenue figure held pending accountant sign-off, unlock condition displayed)
concept.consentClaimRisks: Evidence drawers are the risk surface: they could leak un-cleared voices, the withheld AU$741,111+ revenue figure, raw Xero data, supplier pricing, or bed-owner PII from /bed embeds. Mitigations: claims-ledger.ts is default-deny — a claim renders ONLY if registered, and quote claims must reference a cleared-voices.ts entry or the chip refuses to render (same guard pattern as products.guards.test.ts); revenue ships as a LOCKED claim whose drawer shows only the unlock condition, never the number; Xero evidence is a summary card (date + reconciler + scope), never a ledger export; the embedded bed record uses a bed already public at /bed/[id] with no owner identity; the 50-bed Experiment page states cost/throughput hypotheses only — no health, disease, or savings outcomes anywhere in the ledger schema (no claim category exists for them, structurally enforcing the ceiling); scabies→RHD appears once, explicitly tagged 'the why — never a claimed outcome'. The changelog itself is the final mitigation: any over-claim would be permanently visible as a retraction, which keeps the founders honest by construction.
concept.whyRemembered: A week later the investor doesn't remember a deck — they remember clicking a number and watching it prove itself, and they remember a date. 'Come back 30 September and diff this deck' converts due diligence from something they do TO Goods into something Goods scheduled FOR them; the 50-bed run becomes an appointment, not an assertion. In a pipeline full of decks that say 'trust us', the only one that said 'audit us — here's the button' is the one they forward to their investment committee, because it does the committee's job for them. And it's unfakeable: it only works because Goods actually has a QR bed register, OCR'd invoices, and consent records — the deck is the moat, demonstrated.

ranking.name: The Working Deck — "Trust the register, not the pitch"
ranking.tagline: Their deck is a live query against the same register that runs the company — I watched a number they'd honestly labelled "modelled" flip to "measured" the week after our meeting.

verdicts.strengths: Maps directly onto a catalytic funder's core diligence question (number integrity + governance discipline) rather than decorating the pitch: the claim state machine, signed-rungs-only match thermometer, deliberately withheld revenue figure, and symmetric flip-or-re-base commitment are honesty made structural. The in-meeting QR scan of a real registered bed is primary evidence that collapses traction verification to zero. The per-investor diff banner is the single highest-value mechanism — it arms the internal champion between first meeting and investment committee, exactly where commitments stall against a 31 Aug deadline. Comprehension cost is low because the 13-slide skeleton stays orthodox with the live layer on top. Feasibility is credible: verified in-repo that the deck chassis with the verified/modelled/interest/future label system, LOI ladder (loi-pipeline.ts), canon rollup (asset-canonical.ts, impact-fetcher.ts), cost engine, /bed/[id] pages, and per-slug gating (funder-pages.ts) all already exist, so 3.5 weeks is plausible rather than fantasy. Consent/claims risk handling is unusually rigorous and pre-empts precisely what this funder archetype probes. Genuinely only buildable by this company, which itself evidences the operational claim.
verdicts.weaknesses: The marquee feature never fires before the decision: the 50-bed run starts 15 Sep, so the modelled-to-measured flip lands after the 31 Aug commitment and the tagline's 'I watched it flip' story contradicts the concept's own timeline — I still commit on a modelled $421. The committee decides on the PDF export, where the live layer collapses back into a classic deck with badges. The real diligence bottlenecks — accountant sign-off on the $741K revenue, referee calls on LOI counterparties, 2-founder key-person risk — are unaffected by deck format. 3.5 founder-weeks is roughly half the remaining runway to deadline spent on deck machinery instead of signing rungs; scope creep risk on snapshot tables, embed mode, and changelog emails is high. A stale or negative diff banner ('nothing changed since your last visit') broadcasts stagnation as efficiently as it broadcasts progress. Live demos fail at demo time; a reconciliation glitch mid-meeting inverts the entire trust premise. Much of the persuasive core (honest labels, pre-registered experiment framing) could ship in the classic excellent deck at near-zero cost, so the marginal conviction gain over the baseline is smaller than claimed.

verdicts.strengths: Exceptional reuse honesty — every item in usesExisting was verified in the repo: the /deck chassis already ships the exact verified/modelled/interest/future label system (deck/page.tsx line 34), getCanonicalAssetRollup() exists in impact-fetcher.ts, LOI_RUNGS/STAGE_TO_RUNG/MATCH_TARGET exist in loi-pipeline.ts with stage IDs verified against GHL, funder-pages.ts password gating is in production, /bed/[id] is live, and drift-check scripts (check-asset-drift.mjs, check-canon-drift.mjs) already run. Roughly 70% of the concept is wiring, not building. Gate compliance is structurally pre-baked rather than promised: canon.ts labels the $421 as claimLabel 'modelled', the current deck already tags 200–350 requests as 'interest, not committed revenue', already renders scabies→RHD as 'the why — not a claimed outcome', and all four quote voices (Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Dianne Stokes, Norman Frank) are on the default-deny cleared-voices.ts allowlist. The concept makes the claim ceiling the product — the modelled/measured empty slot is compliance turned into the memorable centrepiece, and live-rendering from canon is the strongest possible form of 'canon numbers only'. Revenue is deliberately NOT wired (held at the canon 741,111 pending accountant sign-off, which matches canon.ts's own amber caveat). The static PDF export covers QBE Stage 2 paper requirements and doubles as meeting-day insurance. Maintenance burden is lower than it looks because unmapped GHL stages drop OFF the ladder (fails toward understatement, never overstatement), and the codebase already has the static-canon fallback pattern for fetch failures. Timeline fits: even at a realistic 5 weeks (vs the claimed 3.5), a 10 July start clears 31 Aug with buffer, and the least essential items (changelog email, QR cards) are the cheapest.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) The scheduled $421 state transition is the sharpest latent gate risk: if implemented as a date-triggered flip, a passed date does not make a number measured — the flip MUST be human-gated on verified run completion; the design implies this ('flip-or-re-base') but doesn't state the enforcement mechanism. (2) Internal inconsistency on the thermometer: slide 12 says 'fed by signed rungs only' while mitigation 1 says Contracted/Cash only — loi-pipeline.ts itself warns a GHL 'Committed' stage is not a signed LOI, so it must resolve to the stricter reading or the match thermometer can overstate secured match. (3) The trust framing has a self-inflicted failure mode: staleness and downtime are visible by design — the register's last reconcile is 2026-05-30, already 6 weeks stale today, so shipping the heartbeat without a weekly reconciliation habit turns the centrepiece into evidence of operational lapse; two founders mid-raise are exactly the people who skip reconciliation. (4) A live deck breaking (Supabase/GHL outage) during an investor meeting is a real scenario; the static fallback must be baked in, not optional. (5) The showcase-bed embed depends on a human consent clearance with no stated code fallback, and /bed/[id] has ~20 components (recipient-card, recipient-panel, story-modal, galleries) whose embed-mode stripping must be regression-tested every release — one leak exposes a recipient to an investor. (6) 3.5 weeks is optimistic; the per-investor snapshot/diff layer (deck_views table, first-open semantics when links are forwarded to ICs) is the most novel, least-reused code and should be the designated scope cut. (7) The flip payoff mostly lands after the decision window: the run starts 15 Sep, Stage 2 is Sept, so QBE likely never witnesses the flip — the empty slot must carry the weight alone.

verdicts.strengths: The core move is genuinely novel: a claim state machine where the deck pre-registers the falsification test of its own headline number — modelled AU$421 beside a visibly empty 'measured' slot with a commitment date, flip-or-re-base bound in copy, symmetry (it can write a worse number) stated on the slide. Pre-registration from science imported into fundraising is not something I've seen in any 'innovative deck'. Two supporting moves are also fresh: withholding rendered as a first-class claim state (revenue 'held at AU$741,111 pending accountant sign-off'), and a physical QR card that resolves to a live asset record inside the deck — earned, not gimmick, because Goods' product is literally QR-traceable ('the product audited itself'). The week-later partner sentence is real and repeatable: 'There was an empty box with a date where the measured cost number goes, and I scanned a card and an actual bed in Tennant Creek answered.' The whole design is an argument aimed precisely at a catalytic funder's diligence question (can I trust their numbers and discipline), and it's structurally only buildable by this company. Verified against the repo: every cited asset exists (v2/src/app/deck/page.tsx with the verified/modelled/interest system, v2/src/lib/data/asset-canonical.ts, cost-model-scenarios.ts, loi-pipeline.ts, funder-pages.ts, cleared-voices.ts, bed/[id] pages), so the 3.5-week estimate extends real plumbing rather than inventing it. Consent/claim risks are handled with hard, specific mitigations (signed-rungs-only thermometer, single consent-flagged showcase bed, revenue deliberately unwired).
verdicts.weaknesses: Roughly 60% of the chassis is standard fare with new paint: gated per-investor microsites, live-metrics decks, interactive model sliders, and 'changed since you last opened' changelogs (Visible.vc-style investor-update tooling relocated into a deck) all exist. The signature payoff has a timeline flaw: the 50-bed run starts 15 Sep, so the claim-flip — the tagline's magic moment — cannot fire before the 31 Aug first-commitment deadline and likely not before Stage 2 either; the empty slot must carry the argument unfilled, which is weaker theatre. The return-visit diff layer depends on investors reopening the link, behavior the deck cannot force (the mitigation is a manual founder email, i.e. a normal investor update). Live wiring cuts both ways: if bed deployments stall between meetings, the deck faithfully displays stagnation. Density risk: 14 slides and five distinct mechanisms (live heartbeat, QR embed, playable model, experiment slide, diff layer) could fragment memorability — the concept is strongest if the experiment slide is unmistakably the centrepiece and the rest recedes. 3.5 weeks is a large spend for a 2-founder team seven weeks out; slippage past early August compresses rehearsal and QBE Stage 2 PDF-export needs.

concept.name: The Working Deck — "Trust the register, not the pitch"
concept.tagline: Their deck is a live query against the same register that runs the company — I watched a number they'd honestly labelled "modelled" flip to "measured" the week after our meeting.
concept.coreInnovation: Claims with expiry dates and a per-investor diff. Every key number in the deck is a live render from the operational data layer (asset register rollup, cost-model engine, LOI ladder) governed by a claim state machine (verified / modelled / interest / future -> measured / re-based). The centrepiece is a pre-registered experiment slide: the AU$421 in-house cost is displayed beside a visibly EMPTY "measured" slot with a commitment date, and when the 50-bed run completes the deck flips the claim live — in the copy the investor already has open. On every return visit, a "since you last opened this" strip diffs the deck against a snapshot taken at their first view: beds +N, ladder rung moved, claim state changed. No other deck changes truthfully between meeting one and meeting two; this one is designed to.
concept.experienceFlow: The investor gets a personal link (`/deck/qbe`, per-slug password, same pattern as `/funders/[slug]`) and, in the meeting, a printed card with a real bed's QR code. The deck opens on the familiar one-liner, but under it beats a heartbeat strip: "496 beds on register · 9 communities · last reconciled 2026-05-30 14:12 AEST" — rendered live from `getCanonicalAssetRollup()`, not typed into a slide. Slide 3 they scan the card: a real `/bed/[id]` record opens inside the deck — manufacture date, delivery event, scan history, map pin. The product just audited itself. On the model slide they drag the price and volume sliders of the embedded cost playground (`useCostModel`) and watch breakeven move; the BUY/MAKE toggle shows why the 50-bed run matters. Then the experiment slide: "$421 is modelled. On completion of the 50-bed run this slot fills with the measured number — or we re-base and tell you." The ask slide shows the QBE match thermometer assembling from the live LOI ladder. Two weeks later they reopen the link: a quiet banner reads "3 things changed since 28 July — 12 beds deployed, one commitment moved Target to Committed, cost claim still modelled (run starts 15 Sep)." They forward the link to their investment committee, because the link is the diligence.
architecture: 1. Cover — same Airbnb one-liner + live heartbeat strip (canon rollup + last-reconciled timestamp from impact-fetcher.ts); establishes 'this deck is wired to the register'
2. Why — Alfred Johnson freight quote (cleared), scabies->RHD framed explicitly as the why, never an outcome; static by design, honesty is the interactive element here
3. Scan a bed — embedded live /bed/[id] record of a consent-flagged showcase bed: event timeline, scan log, Leaflet map; physical QR card handed across the table
4. The bed — X-trestle tension design in 3 steps, specs imported from products.ts, in-repo assembly.mp4 / AssemblySequence component
5. Traction — canon numbers rendered live with verified/interest badges; 200-350 requests labelled interest; drift-check script guarantees deck never contradicts the register
6. Why now — micro-manufacturing, IPP/Closing the Gap (1 July 2026 threshold), HDPE + freight economics; the one classic slide, unchanged
7. The model, playable — embedded CostPlayground (cost-model-context.tsx, invoice-OCR-verified v6 scenarios); investor drags price/beds, flips BUY/MAKE, sees breakeven 1,679 -> ~338
8. The experiment — pre-registered 50-bed run: modelled $421 beside an empty 'measured' slot with a date; copy commits to flip-or-re-base; this is the slide that changes
9. GTM — three wedges with named buyers (Centrecorp, Stokes/PICC, direct+QBE) + eight procurement pipes summarised; live LOI ladder (LOI_RUNGS) shows the round assembling
10. Risks — scored 4-row register incl. revenue held at AU$741,111 pending sign-off; the withheld number is itself displayed as a claim-state ('awaiting accountant')
11. Team + governance triggers — 2 founders, trigger-gated GM (Oct) and BD (Feb 27) hires, independent-director path that clears SEFA's covenant; tagged future
12. Ask — AU$400K recoverable grant converting to community ownership; match thermometer vs MATCH_TARGET fed by signed rungs only; 31 Aug / Sept Stage 2 dates
13. Close — Norman Frank quote (cleared), 'Trust the register, not the pitch', CTA to /investors?skin=investment and /cost-story
14. Return-visit layer (not a slide) — per-viewer snapshot diff banner + optional changelog email; deck version hash in footer; one-click static PDF export for QBE Stage 2 paper requirements
usesExisting: /deck page.tsx slide chassis, keyboard nav, and its verified/modelled/interest/future label system (extended, not replaced)
asset-canonical.ts + getCanonicalAssetRollup() in impact-fetcher.ts (live canon numbers + drift check)
cost-model-scenarios.ts v6 + useCostModel engine + cost-model-context.tsx + CostPlayground/cost-charts from /cost-story

/bed/[id] Supabase-backed record pages + bed-owners.ts QR resolution (goods-tracker.app/support?asset_id=...)

media-pack photos (lying-on-stretch-bed, community-testing-bed-golden-hour, nic-with-elder-on-verandah), pitch/bed-qr-code.jpg, stretch-bed/assembly.mp4

needsBuilding: A claims.ts registry: each headline number declared once with source query, claim state, and (for the $421) a scheduled state transition — the deck renders claims, never literals
Server data routes (/api/deck/rollup, /api/deck/ladder) so the client deck hydrates live numbers without shipping the service key; ladder endpoint exposes signed rungs only
Per-investor instances /deck/[slug]: gate cookie + a Supabase deck_views table storing a numbers-snapshot at first open; diff banner computed against it on return
Match thermometer component driven by MATCH_TARGET and contracted/cash rungs
Consent-flagged 'showcase bed' selection + embed mode for /bed/[id] inside the deck (strip admin widgets, owner identity redacted unless explicitly cleared)
Static export path: a print stylesheet / snapshot-to-PDF route that freezes the live numbers with an as-at timestamp for QBE Stage 2 paper artifacts
Physical QR cards (print run, trivial) linking the showcase bed and the gated deck
Optional changelog email (manual send at first — a founder forwards the diff; automation later)
concept.consentClaimRisks: Four live-data risks, each with a hard mitigation. (1) LOI ladder could imply unsigned money is secured — the /api/deck/ladder endpoint whitelists Contracted/Cash rungs for the thermometer and renders everything below as counts labelled "interest, not committed revenue"; no funder names surface without a signed commitment. (2) The live /bed/[id] embed could expose an owner's identity or an uncleared face — use one admin-designated showcase bed with explicit consent, embed mode strips owner resolution and gallery, and imagery stays media-pack only (no live Empathy Ledger galleries in the deck, since EL exposes no consent flag). (3) The experiment slide could read as pre-announcing success — copy is fixed as "measured, or we re-base honestly," and the flip mechanism can write a WORSE number just as easily; that symmetry is stated on the slide. (4) Live wiring could leak the unreconciled revenue figure — revenue is deliberately NOT wired; it renders as a withheld claim ("held at AU$741,111 until accountant sign-off"), and scabies->RHD stays hard-coded prose (the why), never a queried metric. Quotes limited to the four already-cleared voices in the baseline deck.
concept.whyRemembered: A week later the investor doesn't remember slide layouts — they remember that the deck emailed reality back to them: "12 beds deployed since you read this; one commitment moved to Contracted; the cost number you questioned is still honestly labelled modelled, run starts 15 Sep." They scanned a physical QR card and a real bed in Tennant Creek answered. Every other deck in their inbox is a claim about a company; this one is the company, observable. For a catalytic funder whose whole diligence question is "can I trust their numbers and their governance discipline," a deck that structurally cannot overstate — because it renders from the same register the founders operate from, and publicly schedules the falsification test of its own key claim — is itself the strongest evidence. Only Goods can make it, because only Goods has QR-traceable beds, an invoice-verified cost engine, and a consent architecture already in production.

ranking.name: Bed №497 — The Deck You Can Hold
ranking.tagline: Goods mailed me a chunk of community plastic with a QR code; when I scanned it, my pitch deck turned out to be a live entry in their factory register — and signing the grant writes the next event on that bed's timeline.

verdicts.strengths: Tried hard to refute this as theatre and most of it survived, for one reason: the stunt is backed by verifiable primary evidence. I checked the repo — the per-asset register (/bed/[id] with scan logging and event timeline), the cost-model engine, the LOI pipeline mapping, the consent allowlist, and the funder gating all actually exist, so 'Bed 497 is a reserved row in the real register' is a true statement, not a rendering. That matters to a sceptic: (1) The register-as-traction-slide genuinely shortens diligence — I can scan a real bed record at midnight, see 496 assets across 9 communities, and verify operational reality without a single reference call. It also signals systems maturity far beyond a typical 2-founder outfit, which is a stewardship signal for $400K. (2) The calibrated-claim architecture (FUTURE label on the non-existent bed, MODELLED badges with '0 beds assembled in-house yet', RHD framed as 'our why, not a claimed outcome', no dollar figures on unsigned money) is aimed precisely at a funder's core fear — being oversold — and raises conviction more than any traction chart would. (3) The metaphor is load-bearing, not decorative: canvas-is-structural = community-is-structural, and 'your grant manufactures the cover slide' encodes the recoverable-grant theory of change (money → measured run → MODELLED becomes VERIFIED) correctly and memorably. (4) Self-serve cost sliders pre-empt the 'send me the model in Excel' cycle. (5) Consent risks are pre-mitigated in a way that itself demonstrates the moat — highly relevant to QBE/Snow-type funders in Indigenous contexts. (6) Comprehension is fast, not slowed: the whole conceit fits in one sentence.
verdicts.weaknesses: Does it make me commit FASTER? Only marginally, and several elements cut the other way. (1) Physical-mail latency: it is 10 July with a 31 Aug deadline; kit production plus post adds days to first contact versus an emailable deck, and 3 founder-weeks of build is nearly half the remaining runway for a 2-founder team that still has entity/51% structure pending at MinterEllison — a sceptic reads that resource allocation as a prioritisation red flag. (2) No committee artifact is specified: foundations decide in investment committees on circulated papers; scrollytelling with live event logs doesn't circulate, so someone at QBE has to flatten this into a PDF anyway — the concept optimises the meeting, not the decision process. (3) The theatrical close ('sign, and the commitment is logged') mismatches institutional reality — IC approval, grant-agreement drafting, and signature happen weeks after the pitch, so the deck's designed peak moment cannot occur in the room. (4) It shortens the easy diligence (does the product exist?) while leaving the hard diligence untouched: recoverability waterfall, financials/runway, legal structure, and key-person risk get one honest 'risks, unassembled' beat but no acceleration. (5) The three most persuasive elements — live bed assembly in the room, a link to the live register, MODELLED-badged cost model — are fully severable and could ride on a classic 14-slide deck at ~10% of the effort; the marginal persuasion of the serial-number apparatus over that baseline is smaller than the concept claims. (6) Logging the funder's own scan, even disclosed, will strike some program officers as instrumenting the relationship.

verdicts.strengths: Rare case where the concept is composition, not invention: every single item in usesExisting was verified present in the repo — cleared-voices.ts default-deny allowlist (Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Dianne Stokes all cleared), loi-pipeline.ts with the verbatim 'do not present as secured until awarded' QBE note, the deck's verified/modelled/interest/future pill system, useCostModel engine, assembly-sequence.tsx, trip-story.tsx blocks, funder-pages.ts gating, /bed/[id] with scan logging and bed_journeys timeline. The riskiest move — inserting reserved serial rows into the production assets register — is verifiably safe: both getCanonicalAssetRollup() and scripts/check-asset-drift.mjs filter status='deployed', so №497–499 cannot inflate the canon 496. The claim ceiling is internalised as the aesthetic rather than bolted on: FUTURE is the headline of the physical tag and masthead, MODELLED with '0 beds assembled in-house yet' is a pitch beat (matching existing deck line 340 verbatim), the RHD 'our why, not a claimed outcome' banner is specified, the LOI ladder shows stage names only, and the AU$741,111 hold is respected — compliance survives edit pressure because removing it breaks the story. Scan logging is already privacy-sound (salted IP hash, bot-filtered) and disclosed on the kit card. Crucially it is NOT on the critical path: the existing 698-line /deck ships regardless, so slippage degrades gracefully rather than killing the 31 Aug commitment. The inversion — the money raised manufactures the deck's own cover slide — is genuinely only possible with this company's per-bed provenance infrastructure, and the physical offcut on the funder's desk is a persistent artifact no PDF competes with.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) Timeline: 3 effort-weeks is optimistic for 14 beats + a chrome-free composed route + present mode + 3 per-funder variants + physical kit production; realistic calendar is 4–5 weeks against a 7-week runway (today 10 Jul → 31 Aug), on top of manufacturing, MinterEllison entity work, and grant writing for two founders. Needs a declared cut-line: ship the QBE №497 variant only, drop SEFA/Snow variants and even present-mode if slipping. (2) The №497 framing leans on canon 496 total beds, of which 363 are discontinued Basket Beds and only 133 are Stretch Beds; juxtaposing '496 beds / 2,660kg' invites a funder to compute 5.4kg/bed against the claimed 20kg/bed — the stretch/basket split must be displayed beside the rollup or a diligence-savvy funder (SEFA is a lender) unpicks the theatre. (3) 'No free navigation into the wider register' is not actually enforceable once you deep-link the public /bed/GB0-156-40 page — it renders sibling beds, an all-assets map, and recipient_name; defensible only because those pages are already on the open web, so the design should claim 'no new exposure' rather than containment. (4) The whole concept hinges on live infrastructure performing in the meeting — GHL LOI fetch and the live scan-log write need cached/static fallbacks, because a mid-pitch fetch failure falsifies the core claim ('the register is live') in front of the funder. (5) 'Sign → event logged' is a founder doing a manual admin write, and the post-run promise (measured cost sheet + №497 delivery record with cleared imagery) is an unsystematised follow-through obligation with no reminder mechanism — a quiet integrity liability if the 50-bed run slips. (6) Minor copy drift: engine comments say ~$426 Factory vs the deck's $421; live sliders render engine truth, but static copy should be reconciled once.

verdicts.strengths: The individual ingredients are all standard fare — lumpy mail with a QR code, personalized funder microsites, embedded live dashboards, interactive cost sliders, scrollytelling, a live product demo in the room. I have seen every one of them. But the core move is not the ingredients; it is the ontology: the deck is not ABOUT the product, it IS a row in the company's real production asset register, sharing a data model with 496 physically-existing beds. Reserving serial №497 with an honest FUTURE label, logging the funder's own scan as the first event, logging their signed commitment as the second, and closing the loop when the funded run manufactures the bed itself — that recursion ('the deck's cover slide gets built by the money it raises') is a move I have not seen executed. Charity:water's post-hoc well provenance and Kickstarter backer numbers are cousins, but pre-hoc reservation of a provenance record as the pitch substrate is new. The partner's sentence a week later is specific and repeatable: 'They mailed me a chunk of the actual recycled plastic with the serial number of a bed that doesn't exist yet — the pitch is that bed's live register page, and our grant literally manufactures it.' The metaphor mapping is also unusually earned rather than cute: the canvas is literally structural to the bed and community is literally structural to the enterprise, so the parts-to-claims structure isn't decoration. The honesty engineering (FUTURE labels, MODELLED badges, stage-names-only LOI ladder, held revenue staying held) converts the claim ceiling from a constraint into the aesthetic. Only a company with a live per-bed provenance register could run this play — it is a moat demo disguised as a deck. Repo verification confirms the reuse list is real, making 3 weeks credible for a 2-founder team against 31 Aug.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) Channel risk: physical mail to institutional funders (QBE Foundation, SEFA) often dies in a mailroom or EA's desk; if the decision-maker never holds the token, slide one of the deck evaporates and /deck/497 opens cold. Needs a hand-delivery or in-meeting-reveal fallback. (2) Scan-logging the funder, even disclosed on the card, can read as surveillance to a risk-averse philanthropic committee — the very moment meant to prove the register is live could produce a flinch instead. (3) The present-mode gimmick (one beat advances per physical part placed while a founder assembles a real bed) is fragile theater; a fumbled pole or a laggy keystroke turns the room's attention to the mechanics. It also demands one founder perform assembly while the other pitches — the full 2-person team consumed by choreography. (4) A literal-minded grant committee can invert the framing: 'you sent us a paperweight and a promise' — the non-existence headline is bold but hands skeptics their opening line. (5) Writing pitch-flow events ('commitment received') into the production assets table mixes fundraising state into operational data; status:reserved mitigates but serial-collision handling (real beds registered during the raise) is unaddressed. (6) 14 architecture items plus three per-funder serial variants is a lot of surface for 3 weeks; the physical kit production day and the consent guard on the exemplar bed record are the two items most likely to slip or be cut, and cutting either weakens the whole conceit.

concept.name: Bed №497 — The Deck You Can Hold
concept.tagline: Goods mailed me a chunk of community plastic with a QR code; when I scanned it, my pitch deck turned out to be a live entry in their factory register — and signing the grant writes the next event on that bed's timeline.
concept.coreInnovation: The deck is a reserved serial number in Goods' real asset register. Canon says 496 beds exist; the funder is handed Bed №497 — a bed that does not exist yet, honestly labelled FUTURE — and the pitch is that bed's provenance page (/deck/497), built on the same Supabase `assets` infrastructure as every live /bed/GB0-xxx record. The physical kit (a pressed-HDPE factory offcut token + silk-screened canvas swatch carrying the QR) is slide one; the funder's signed commitment becomes a logged event on the bed's timeline; the 50-bed run that their money funds is the run that makes Bed №497 real. No other deck ends by manufacturing its own cover slide.
concept.experienceFlow: A small box arrives: a ~200g pressed-HDPE offcut (real factory offcut — the same ones weighed in the `/images/process/` shots), a heavy-canvas swatch silk-screened with "We press beds On Country, and build the factory so community owns it," and a QR tag reading GB0-497. A card says, honestly: "This plastic is real. The bed isn't — yet." They scan it. The page (/deck/497, chrome-free like /deck) opens on the masthead: "This is Bed №497. It doesn't exist yet. Here's why it will." Their own scan appears in the record's event log — proof the register is live, felt viscerally. Then the pitch unfolds as the bed's assembly: five parts, five claims, five minutes — X-legs (the plastic loop), poles (the unit economics, with the real CostPlayground sliders, badged MODELLED), canvas (community voices, structural — Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Dianne Stokes, all cleared), tension (the round assembling live via the LOI ladder against the QBE $400K match, 31 Aug clock visible), assembled bed (the ask: $400K recoverable grant converting to community ownership). At the live meeting, a founder assembles an actual Stretch Bed in the room while presenting — present-mode advances one beat per part placed. Close: sign, and "commitment received" is written to Bed №497's timeline. When the 50-bed run completes, they receive the measured cost sheet and №497's delivery record.
architecture: 1. PHYSICAL KIT (slide zero): pressed-HDPE offcut token + silk-screened canvas swatch + QR tag GB0-497, with an honesty card — 'this plastic is real; the bed isn't, yet' (FUTURE label printed on the tag itself)

2. SCAN → /deck/497 masthead: 'This is Bed №497. It doesn't exist yet.' — trip-story masthead block, media-pack/lying-on-stretch-bed.jpg; the funder's own scan appears live in the event log (proves the register is real)
3. FLOOR: the problem — Alfred Johnson (Palm Island, cleared) freight quote; scabies→RHD shown under an explicit 'this is our why, not a claimed outcome' banner
4. PARTS ON THE GROUND: existing assembly-sequence.tsx with pitch/bed-seq-1..3.jpg + stretch-bed/assembly.mp4 — 5 parts, 5 minutes, no tools = the structure of the next 5 beats

5. THE REGISTER IS THE TRACTION SLIDE: live getCanonicalAssetRollup() (496 beds / 9 communities / 16 washers / 2,660kg) + a deep link into one real hand-picked bed record (e.g. /bed/GB0-156-40) with its scan log and map
6. X-LEGS = THE LOOP: process/ shredder→press→CNC photo run; 20kg HDPE per bed; the on-country plant that moves to community ownership

7. POLES = THE MONEY: embedded CostPlayground on the shared useCostModel() engine — funder drags price/volume sliders themselves; $685→$421 hard-badged MODELLED with '0 beds assembled in-house yet; Bed №497's run is the measurement'

8. CANVAS = COMMUNITY, STRUCTURAL: the bed won't stand without the canvas and the enterprise won't stand without community — Ivy, Dianne Stokes quotes (cleared-voices.ts); Empathy Ledger consent architecture shown as the moat itself

9. TENSION = THE ROUND: public-safe LOI ladder from loi-pipeline.ts (Target→Committed→Contracted→Cash) visualising the QBE 1:1 match as the tensioning strap, 31 Aug deadline on-screen; stage names only, nothing unsigned called 'secured'

10. THE ASK, AS A PARTS LIST: AU$400K recoverable grant converting to community ownership — 50-bed run $60-80K, GM+BD ~$200K, press capex $110,046 VERIFIED — each line tagged with the existing verified/modelled/future pills
11. RISKS, UNASSEMBLED: Hoffman slide as an honest exploded diagram — cost-down unproven, 0 hires, entity/51% pending MinterEllison, revenue held pending sign-off

12. SIGN → EVENT LOGGED: on commitment, 'commitment received' is written to Bed №497's asset timeline; funder is promised the measured cost sheet + №497's delivery record (cleared imagery only) when the run completes
13. PRESENT-MODE (/deck/497?present=1): keyboard chrome reused from /deck; pitched while a real Stretch Bed is assembled in the meeting room — one beat advances per part placed

14. PER-FUNDER SERIALS: reuse funder-pages.ts per-slug cookie gating — QBE gets №497, SEFA №498 (with the governance-covenant beat swapped in), Snow №499 (letter-of-support role beat); each funder literally holds a different bed

/deck keyboard/progress chrome and the verified/modelled/interest/future label system

process/, media-pack/, people/ photo sets; real factory HDPE offcuts for the physical token
needsBuilding: /deck/[serial] route composing trip-story blocks + cost-model context + bed-record fetch into one chrome-free page, with ?present=1 keyboard mode
Reserved-serial rows in the assets table (status: reserved) + a 'commitment received' event type, so №497-499 are real register entries labelled FUTURE
Public-safe LOI ladder component (stage names only, no dollar leakage for unsigned money) reading loi-pipeline.ts
Per-funder beat variants (QBE / SEFA / Snow) config in funder-pages.ts schema
Physical kit production: ~5 offcut tokens with engraved/stickered QR, silk-screened canvas swatches, honesty card — a founder afternoon at the factory partner
Consent guard on the deep-linked exemplar bed record: hand-picked, cleared-imagery-only, no uncleared names
concept.consentClaimRisks: Four live risks, each with a designed mitigation. (1) Bed №497 does not exist — mitigated by making non-existence the headline: the tag, the card, and the masthead all carry the FUTURE label; the offcut is real, the bed is explicitly conditional on the run. (2) Deep links into live /bed records could surface uncleared faces or names — the exemplar record is hand-picked, and the deck route renders only media-pack/cleared imagery via the cleared-voices.ts and canon-el-picks consent flags; no free navigation into the wider register. (3) The live LOI ladder could imply unsigned money is secured — it shows stage names from loi-pipeline.ts only, inherits the interest/future pills, and never renders a dollar figure for anything pre-contract; the held AU$741,111 revenue figure stays held. (4) Scabies→RHD and cost-down over-claim — the RHD banner states 'our why, not a claimed outcome' verbatim, and every cost number in the embedded playground carries the MODELLED badge with '0 beds assembled in-house yet' until the run replaces it with measured data. Investor scan-logging is disclosed on the kit card.
concept.whyRemembered: A week later there is still a piece of community plastic sitting on their desk, and it is not a memento — it is slide one of a deck that is technically an unfinished bed. They remember that their own scan showed up in the register, that they dragged the cost sliders themselves, and that Goods' answer to 'where's the proof?' was to hand them the serial number of the proof and ask them to fund its manufacture. The deck's cover slide gets built by the money it raises — that inversion is unforgettable, and only a company with a live per-bed provenance register could pull it off.

ranking.name: The Deal Room — a term sheet you assemble, not a deck you watch
ranking.tagline: Goods didn't pitch me a deck — they handed me the controls to their capital stack, and I walked out with my own name on a draft term sheet timed to their 31 August match deadline.

verdicts.strengths: Genuinely shortens diligence rather than decorating it: (1) the additionality question — the ONE question catalytic capital exists to answer — is computed live (my cheque unlocks the QBE match, closes a trough present in all three scenarios, funds the 50-bed proof gate, moves the ownership-transfer date), which no static deck can do; (2) proof is self-verifiable — stat tiles click through to real /bed/[id] register pages I can scan with my own phone, replacing 'trust our numbers' with 'audit our register', and I verified these pages exist in the codebase with scan logs, maps and event timelines; (3) radical honesty is structural, not rhetorical — '$0 signed today' shown plainly, verified/modelled badges on every figure including inside the builder, match meter counting only signed instruments, '0 beds assembled in-house' stated on the cost slide, four scored risks — this pre-answers the exact gotchas my analyst would spend three weeks surfacing; (4) the term-sheet skeleton compresses institutional process, not just emotion — it hands my program officer the memo skeleton (amount, instrument, milestones, leverage) so the follow-up call starts transactional; (5) the meta-signal is legitimate diligence input: a two-founder team that shipped a live, register-backed, honestly-labelled deal instrument credibly can run a 50-bed production experiment; (6) feasibility is real, not aspirational — funder-pages.ts per-slug schema, loi-pipeline.ts Target/Committed/Contracted/Cash ladder, useCostModel engine, cleared-voices allowlist and /deck shell all verified present, making 3 weeks plausible against a 7-week runway; (7) per-slug instrument defaults (SEFA sub-debt, Snow grant) show the builder respects that different funders transact differently; (8) novelty is concentrated in one slide that IS the transaction — the other twelve are a disciplined, classic, evidence-first deck, so comprehension speed is not sacrificed.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) The climax carries real backfire risk: auto-rendering MY name onto a draft term sheet can read as a pressure tactic to a conservative trustee or credit committee — the DRAFT — NON-BINDING watermark mitigates but the timeshare-tactic perception is a live hazard, especially for grant-side funders less fluent in deal mechanics; (2) disclosed telemetry chills the very exploration it enables — knowing Ben receives my exact configuration, I may decline to interact or my exploratory low-ball gets misread as a signal, and some ICs would instruct officers not to touch it pre-approval; (3) the decision is made by a committee reading a memo, not the person who dragged the block — the experience converts one champion, and the concept under-invests in the artifact the champion carries into the room (the printable PDF helps but is the least-developed component); (4) a meaningful share of the honesty payload (badges, risk rows, honest ladder) could be delivered in an excellent classic deck, so the marginal value of interactivity is concentrated in the builder and register click-throughs — if the drag interaction is at all janky on a trustee's iPad, the marginal value goes negative fast; (5) three founder-weeks consumed inside the critical fundraising window is real opportunity cost for a 2-person team also preparing a 50-bed run and a Stage 2 September; (6) '$0 signed today' plus an interactive first-mover framing puts the anchoring burden entirely on the QBE match — if a funder's read is 'nobody else has signed and they built software instead of closing', the transparency cuts against them; (7) scope-creep risk in needsBuilding (drag physics, print CSS, Supabase logging, per-funder config) is the classic way a 3-week estimate becomes five and eats the deadline.

verdicts.strengths: Exceptionally honest reuse claims — verified ~70% of the surface already exists in the codebase: funder-pages.ts per-slug password gating, the /deck LabelKind (verified/modelled/interest/future) badge system, the tested client-side useCostModel engine with locked numbers, loi-pipeline.ts GHL ladder + MATCH_TARGET, canonical asset rollup, rich /bed/[id] register pages, and even the investment-skin.tsx it cites. Every number is canon and checks out ($685/$426/$421, breakeven 1,679→338, -$171K Nov 2026 trough present in all three Day 9 scenarios, $150K floor/$400K cap, 31 Aug signed-LOI gate). All four named voices (Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Dianne Stokes, Norman Frank) are on the default-deny cleared-voices.ts allowlist. The design bakes honesty mechanisms in rather than bolting them on: $0-signed shown plainly, DRAFT—NON-BINDING watermark, disclosed telemetry, modelled badges inside the builder, scabies→RHD following the deck's existing 'the why — not a claimed outcome' pattern. The 7 weeks to 31 Aug is workable, a clean de-scope path exists (drop cheque-timing from the trough chart), and maintenance is low: dealConfig per new funder is a data edit, the LOI ladder is already live from GHL. The meta-claim (a two-founder team shipping this proves execution capability) is genuinely persuasive.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) The emotional core — 'watch your cheque double through the QBE match' — runs straight at canon's hard line: MATCH_TARGET.note says do NOT present QBE funding as secured; it is Steering Committee discretion, outcomes Nov 2026. A mechanically-filling meter implies mechanical leverage; it must say 'unlocks match ELIGIBILITY' and the drama fights the disclaimer. Worse, the $150K-floor math encodes an answer to the floor-aggregation question the QBE playbook explicitly lists as OPEN (can sub-floor grants aggregate?). (2) The trough chart wired to cheque size AND timing is a mini cashflow model presented to the exact user most able to break it — a professional lender. Sub-debt repayments should deepen later months; the curve derives from revenue assumptions the page simultaneously withholds pending Standard Ledger sign-off. Naive add-cheque-at-month-X math will be caught on the call it's meant to win. (3) 3 weeks is optimistic; the timing-wired chart pushes it to 4, and July–Aug founder attention belongs to selling, not reviewing widget math. (4) Hardcoded Day 8/9 scenario curves will silently drift once Standard Ledger signs off — needs a re-base plan and ideally a drift check like check-asset-drift.mjs. (5) A document titled 'term sheet' with the funder's name, even watermarked, can trigger funder-side compliance allergy; 'draft position summary' is safer. (6) The 31 Aug countdown needs a post-deadline state or the page turns stale on 1 Sept.

verdicts.strengths: The core move — the funder assembles their own cheque into the issuer's real, invoice-verified capital stack and the deck ends by rendering a draft term sheet with their name and numbers on it — is genuinely unseen in the deck genre; calculators and doc-gen exist in fintech invest flows (AngelList, syndication portals), but never as the narrative climax of a fundraise where every prior slide exists to earn the handover of controls. It produces the exact memorable sentence a partner repeats a week later: 'They didn't send me a deck — I built my own term sheet on their site and the founder called about the numbers I picked.' The artifact persistence (a PDF with the funder's own name) converts the follow-up call from re-pitching to papering. It is deadline-native (QBE 31 Aug match is the mechanic, not a footnote) and proves the meta-claim that a two-founder team ships. Reuse claims were verified against the repo: funder-pages.ts, loi-pipeline.ts, cost-model workspace + investment skin, /bed/[id] register pages, /deck shell, cleared-voices consent gate all exist, making the 3-week estimate credible rather than aspirational. Honesty engineering (LabelKind badges inside the builder, $0-signed shown plainly, NON-BINDING watermark, disclosed telemetry) is unusually thorough for this genre.
verdicts.weaknesses: The interaction is three parameters (amount, instrument, timing) dressed as drag-and-drop — if the physicality is thin it degrades to a skinned form and the novelty collapses to 'investment calculator with mail-merge.' The ownership-transfer date sliding left with cheque size is the riskiest claim element: transfer actually gates on the MinterEllison entity decision, GM hire, and the 50-bed proof, not linearly on dollars — animating deterministic causality there brushes the claim ceiling even with badges, and needs an explicit 'indicative, milestone-gated' treatment. The honest empty match meter ($0 signed today) deflates the centerpiece's drama on first load. Some institutional funders will read an auto-drafted term sheet as presumptuous or legally uncomfortable, and configuration telemetry, even disclosed, can feel like being watched. Slides 1-7 are a conventional (if well-evidenced) deck — the concept has one great room and eleven ordinary corridors. 3 weeks is the top of plausible for two founders against the 31 Aug deadline; the term-sheet print/PDF path and the Supabase logging endpoint are the classic underestimated tails.

concept.name: The Deal Room — a term sheet you assemble, not a deck you watch
concept.tagline: Goods didn't pitch me a deck — they handed me the controls to their capital stack, and I walked out with my own name on a draft term sheet timed to their 31 August match deadline.
concept.coreInnovation: The deck's climax is a drag-and-drop capital-stack builder wired to the real, invoice-verified cost model and the live LOI commitment register: the funder physically places their cheque (size, instrument, timing) into Goods' actual stack and watches four consequences recompute live — QBE 1:1 match leverage, the all-scenario cash trough closing, the 50-bed proof gate getting funded, and the community-ownership transfer date moving earlier — then the deck ends by rendering that exact configuration as a non-binding term sheet skeleton with the funder's name, their numbers, and the milestones they just funded. No other deck models the transaction itself; every slide before it exists only to earn the right to hand over the controls.
concept.experienceFlow: Hannah at SEFA opens /deal/sefa (per-slug password, funder-pages pattern). The cover greets her by name, states the one line and the AU$400K mechanism, and shows a live countdown to 31 August. Three keystrokes in, she's not reading claims — she's clicking a stat tile ("496 beds") that opens a real /bed/GB0-156-40 register page with its scan log and event timeline: trust the register, not the pitch. The cost slide is the actual invoice-OCR-verified model, every number badged verified or modelled, with the honest line "0 beds assembled in-house — the 50-bed run is the test." Then the trough chart: the cash dip that appears in all three scenarios, framed as the thing her capital exists to bridge. Then the room changes: "Build your position." She drags a $300K sub-debt block into the stack. The QBE match meter fills past the $150K floor; the trough shallows from -$171K; the 50-bed gate lights "funded"; the ownership-transfer marker slides left. Beside her block sit the other stack layers, honestly labelled Target/Committed/Contracted/Cash from the live LOI ladder. Final screen: a printable term sheet skeleton — SEFA, $300K, recoverable sub-debt, milestones she just funded, marked DRAFT — NON-BINDING — and one button: book the call. Ben gets an email with her exact configuration.
