concept.consentClaimRisks: Biggest risk is the instrument itself implying commitment or over-claiming: the stack builder must render the funder's block in a distinct 'YOUR DRAFT POSITION — not a commitment' style, the match meter must count only signed instruments toward 'match-eligible' (LOI ladder labels Target/Committed honestly, showing $0 signed today rather than hiding it), and the term sheet carries a persistent DRAFT — NON-BINDING — FOR DISCUSSION watermark so no funder feels trapped and no claim of 'secured' money is ever displayed. Cost-down stays badged MODELLED everywhere including inside the builder; the trough chart shows scenarios, not promises; revenue figure stays withheld pending Standard Ledger sign-off; scabies→RHD appears once, explicitly as the why. Voices restricted to the cleared-voices.ts allowlist (Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Dianne Stokes, Norman Frank) and media-pack/process imagery only. Configuration telemetry is disclosed on-page ('we'll see what you build so the call starts where you left off') to avoid covert tracking of a funder. QBE credit line ('Catalysing Impact, powered by Social Impact Hub, in partnership with QBE Foundation') rendered on the match-meter slide.
concept.whyRemembered: A week later the funder doesn't remember slides — they remember the physical act of dropping their cheque into the stack and watching it double through the QBE match, close a cash trough, and pull a community-ownership date closer; and they still have a PDF with their own name and numbers on it, which turns the follow-up call from 'any questions?' into 'shall we paper what you already built?' It also proves the meta-claim: a two-founder team that ships a live, honest, register-backed deal instrument is obviously capable of running a 50-bed production experiment.

ranking.name: The Register: A Deck That Assembles Like the Bed
ranking.tagline: They sent me a deck where every number pulls through to its invoice or a live database row, you can scan a slide and land on a real bed in Tennant Creek, and the last page is the round itself assembling in real time with a slot drawn for us.

verdicts.strengths: Tried to refute it and mostly failed on the core claim. (1) It attacks the actual diligence bottleneck for a two-founder social enterprise: I don't trust their numbers and can't afford to commission verification for a $400K recoverable grant. Pull-through to OCR'd invoices, a live asset register with drift-check dates, and per-number MODELLED/VERIFIED/INTEREST labels replace two or three trust-building meetings — that is a genuine time-to-conviction reduction, not theatre. (2) The pre-registered 50-bed test with published pass/fail criteria and an empty measured-cost axis is the single best element: it reframes the $400K as funding a falsifiable experiment with pre-committed publication 'whatever it says', which is exactly how a recoverable-grant committee wants downside framed. (3) The live LOI ladder with honest stage labels answers 'who else is moving' — the top co-funder validation question for catalytic capital — without inflating unsigned money. (4) Showing the cash trough in all three scenarios answers 'why this instrument' unprompted. (5) The deck IS the product demo: the company's thesis is traceable, honest data infrastructure, and the QR-provenance click-through proves the moat rather than describing it — the one situation where a wildcard interactive deck beats a classic deck plus data room. (6) The board PDF leave-behind shows they understand the real decision happens with zero founders in the room. (7) Consent architecture is structural (build-time lint, no pull-thread on the health 'why'), not a disclaimer. (8) Verified in-repo: nearly all heavy infrastructure already exists, so 4 weeks against a 31 Aug deadline is credible.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) Backfire risk on the close: a countdown clock and a pre-drawn slot with my name on it reads as scarcity sales pressure to an institutional foundation; some trustees will find it presumptuous — needs a per-funder toggle to soften. (2) Live-data fragility: a broken thread-pull, a stale Supabase rollup, or a GHL hiccup in front of a board member converts the deck's entire epistemic premise into evidence against the company; needs snapshot fallbacks. (3) The magic moments only happen to the champion — the forwarded PDF is inert, so the committee experiences a conventional deck with unusual labels; most of the interactivity's value stops at one person. (4) Assembly metaphor headers (LEGS/POLES/CANVAS) impose a small comprehension tax on cold readers, though the canvas-is-structural mapping earns its keep. (5) Invoice confidentiality unexamined: showing actual supplier invoices to third parties may breach supplier terms or leak pricing. (6) Bed map granularity: even voice-free, plotting bed locations in named remote Indigenous communities for funder viewing should be community-level, not household-level — not explicitly addressed. (7) Opportunity cost: 4 weeks of scarce founder/dev time in the exact window the entity decision (MinterEllison, end July) and Standard Ledger sign-off land; a classic deck plus data-room link delivers perhaps 80% of the value at 10% of the effort, and that counterfactual is the honest ceiling on the score.

verdicts.strengths: Unusually honest reuse map — I verified nearly every 'usesExisting' claim in the repo and they are all real: /deck LabelKind pills, useCostModel + CostPlayground, asset-canonical.ts + getCanonicalAssetRollup with an existing drift-check script, loi-pipeline.ts GHL stage mapping, /bed/[id] provenance pages, /funders/[slug] per-slug gating, cleared-voices.ts default-deny allowlist, products.guards.test.ts as a lint pattern. That makes the 4-week estimate credible rather than fictional. The claim-ceiling architecture is the strongest of any concept shape I could stress: MODELLED hard-paired to $421 with a pre-registered empty measured axis (publishing win-or-lose is a genuinely differentiating credibility move), demand explicitly INTEREST, the health 'why' deliberately given NO pull-thread with an on-page note that outcomes are not claimed — that last detail shows the designer understands the scabies-to-RHD drift risk structurally, not cosmetically. The build-time consent lint extends an existing test pattern, so consent enforcement is code, not vigilance — exactly right for a zero-staff team. The leave-behind PDF ('forwarded with zero founders in the room') is the single highest-leverage feature for a 2-person team that cannot attend every board meeting. Canon numbers check out: 496 beds (reconciled 2026-05-30), AU$110,046 press capex (already labelled verified in /deck), invoice-OCR cost model v6 is real.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) The bed-page deep-link breaks the consent design as written: /bed/[id] renders recipientName with no isClearedForExternal filtering (only /stories, /gallery, /community use the filter today), and community-beds.tsx cross-links to sibling beds — so 'link only machine-checkable voice-free beds' fails one click in. Fixable (filter recipient surfaces, or embed a filtered provenance card in /case instead of deep-linking) but it is unbuilt work the estimate does not include. (2) 'Opens the actual OCR'd invoice' — no invoice images exist as web assets in v2/public; the OCR lives in act-infra scripts. Sourcing, redacting (supplier bank details = fraud vector; supplier pricing = confidentiality), and hosting ~30 provenance artifacts is content work that lands on founders, not the AI agent. (3) Live-register empty-state failure mode: before 31 Aug the register IS mostly empty — a live close slide showing '$0 committed' on a funder's boardroom projector broadcasts weak traction in real time; needs a gate that only enables the register beat once >=1 commitment exists. (4) loi-pipeline.ts itself warns a GHL 'Committed' stage is not necessarily a signed LOI — rendering that rung to external funders can imply signed money unless the desc caveat renders with it; same for the QBE 'match unlock' toggle, which must carry the existing 'not secured until awarded, Steering Committee discretion, outcomes Nov 2026' note or it overstates. (5) Timeline pressure: 7.5 weeks to 31 Aug means the artifact must ship in ~3 weeks to be useful in meetings, during which the founders should be fundraising, not QA'ing a 14-beat interactive experience with live GHL/Supabase dependencies — every funder view is a production surface. (6) Naming funders at their true ladder stage to other funders requires a consent-to-be-named flag in GHL that does not exist yet — small but real ops overhead.

verdicts.strengths: The core move clears the novelty bar, and I say that having sat through every flavour of interactive deck. Two elements are genuinely things I have not seen. First, claim-level provenance: every number is a pullable thread that resolves to a primary source — an OCR'd invoice, a live database row with its drift-check date, or a physical bed's QR provenance page in an actual community. Data rooms and decks have always been separate artifacts; fusing them at the level of the individual claim, and having the chain terminate in a physical object you could walk up to and scan, is new. Critically, it is not paint — it is exposing structure the company already built (verified in the repo: supplier-cost-actuals, asset-canonical, /bed/[id], the LabelKind pill system), which is why no competitor deck can copy it. Second, the pre-registered experiment: publishing pass/fail criteria and an empty chart axis where the measured cost will land 'whatever it says' imports scientific pre-registration into fundraising. That is the exact sentence a partner repeats to a colleague a week later: 'They pulled up the actual invoice when I clicked the capex number, and there's a blank chart where they'll publish the real per-unit cost after the 50-bed run — win or lose.' The metaphor is also earned rather than decorative — 'the canvas is structural' maps precisely onto 'community is structural,' and the case literally only standing under tension (their capital) is the rare pitch metaphor that is also the physics of the product. The consent architecture is the strongest of any concept format: a build-time lint that fails the build on an uncleared voice is a structural guarantee, not a checklist. The honesty inversions (visible redaction, MODELLED tags, the cash trough shown in all scenarios, ladder labels that never say 'secured') convert skepticism into propulsion — the deck gets stronger the harder a diligence-minded catalytic funder pushes on it, which is exactly the audience.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) Scope versus the clock is the real threat: 14 beats, ~7 new components, ~30 wired pull-threads, a consent lint, and a print-PDF route in a claimed 4 weeks, from a 2-founder team also running a factory, against a 31 Aug deadline. Four weeks lands ~7 Aug, leaving three weeks of useful life — workable but brittle. And this format is uniquely intolerant of partial delivery: in a normal deck a broken link is a typo; in a deck whose entire thesis is auditability, one dead thread is an integrity failure that poisons every other number. It should ship with 6 bulletproof threads, not 30 aspirational ones. (2) The live commitment register has a cold-start problem the concept doesn't address: an empty ladder in mid-July broadcasts 'nobody has committed' and inverts the urgency mechanic into anti-social-proof. It needs an explicit pre-first-commitment rendering rule. (3) The countdown clock and pre-drawn named slot are the least novel elements — 'your logo here' personalization and deadline timers are decade-old sales furniture — and they carry tonal friction: pressure-tactic theatre sitting inside an artifact whose whole ethos is 'we will never oversell you a number.' Sophisticated catalytic funders may smell the salesmanship. (4) Interaction risk: most reviewers never click. If the threads go unpulled, this degrades gracefully to a good honest deck, but the board-forwarded PDF — the artifact that actually circulates — necessarily flattens the one thing that makes the concept remarkable; the QR-back-to-live-register mitigates but does not solve it. (5) Roughly a third of the architecture (gated per-slug links, metaphor-sequenced beats, embedded playground) is standard interactive-deck fare with excellent paint, not innovation.

concept.name: The Register: A Deck That Assembles Like the Bed
concept.tagline: They sent me a deck where every number pulls through to its invoice or a live database row, you can scan a slide and land on a real bed in Tennant Creek, and the last page is the round itself assembling in real time with a slot drawn for us.
concept.coreInnovation: The deck stops being a description of the company and becomes an auditable interface to its evidence: every claim is a pullable thread that resolves to a primary source (invoice-OCR row, live Supabase asset record, a physical bed's QR provenance page, a canon file with its reconciliation date), the 50-bed cost test is pre-registered on-page with an empty axis where the measured number will be published win or lose, and the closing slide is the live commitment register from the GHL LOI pipeline with the viewing funder's own empty slot pre-drawn. No other deck can do this because no other furniture startup has a QR-traceable provenance graph for every unit shipped, an invoice-verified cost model with per-number confidence flags, and a formal consent architecture for every voice quoted.
concept.experienceFlow: The funder opens their personalised gated link (/case/qbe, /case/sefa, /case/snow — same per-slug cookie pattern as /funders). The opening screen shows the investment case laid out flat like the bed's flat-pack over the assembly.mp4 loop: "This case assembles in five minutes. No tools. Every part load-bearing." They move through it as an assembly sequence — legs (the factory), poles (the economics), canvas (community, which is structural, not decorative), tension (demand and capital). Early on they notice numbers have a small thread icon. They pull one: the AU$110,046 press capex opens the actual OCR'd invoice. They pull "496 beds" and get the live Supabase rollup with its drift-check date, then click into a real bed's public /bed/[id] page — scan log, delivery timeline, map. Skepticism starts working for the deck instead of against it. The economics beat is the live cost playground: they drag the sliders themselves and see the $421 figure wearing its MODELLED tag next to an empty chart axis labelled "measured cost, published here after the 50-bed run, whatever it says." The risks beat shows the cash trough in all three scenarios and a literally redacted revenue figure with the reason. The final screen is the live round: LOI ladder stages (target/committed/contracted/cash, never "secured"), the QBE 1:1 match mechanics as an interactive toggle, a 31 August countdown, and one empty slot with their name on it. One click generates a dated, provenance-stamped board PDF — designed to be forwarded with zero founders in the room.

concept.consentClaimRisks: Four live-data risks, each with a structural mitigation. (1) Live surfaces can leak uncleared voices: /bed/[id] pages and EL galleries aren't consent-filtered for funder context — mitigate by having BedRegister link only to bed records whose content is machine-checkable as voice-free (QR id, community, timeline, map) and by the build-time consent lint asserting every /case quote and image resolves through cleared-voices.ts and allowlisted directories. (2) The live commitment register could imply unsigned money is secured — mitigate by rendering only real GHL stages with the ladder's own labels, never summing unsigned amounts into a 'raised' figure, and never naming funders who haven't consented to be named (show anonymised slots otherwise). (3) The cost-down number gains false authority from interactivity — mitigate by hard-pairing every $421/$426 render with the MODELLED tag and the empty measured axis, so the interface itself says 'hypothesis awaiting the 50-bed run'. (4) Scabies-to-RHD drift: the pull-thread pattern must never let a health 'why' resolve to anything resembling outcome evidence — the why beat gets no thread icon at all, deliberately and visibly, with a one-line note that health outcomes are not claimed. Revenue stays redacted until Standard Ledger signs one Goods-only figure; the redaction is shown, not hidden.
concept.whyRemembered: A week later the funder doesn't remember slides — they remember what they did: pulled a capex number and landed on the actual invoice, clicked a dot on a map and read the scan history of a real bed in a real community, saw a company pre-commit to publishing its measured cost 'whatever it says' next to an empty axis, saw the cash trough nobody else shows, and saw an empty slot in a live round with their name on it and a 31 August countdown. It converts the two things catalytic funders never get from a deck — verifiability and urgency — into physical actions they performed themselves. And the metaphor locks it in: like the bed, the case only stands under tension, and their capital is the tension. That is the sentence they repeat to their board, attached to a PDF the deck generated for exactly that purpose.

ranking.name: The Same Road, Twice
ranking.tagline: The investor rides the freight route a bed travels today and watches the cost meter climb to a verified $685 — then rides the same road back with the factory On Country and watches it fall to a modelled $421 — and the deck ends not with a slide but with their own phone scanning a real bed in the live register.

verdicts.strengths: Tried to refute it and much of it survived. (1) The freight meter is the rare animation that IS the argument rather than decoration — this business case is literally freight economics, so watching distance and verified dollars accumulate together genuinely accelerates comprehension of the unit economics I'd otherwise reconstruct from a spreadsheet. (2) The elements that actually shorten my diligence are real and verified in-repo: an invoice-OCR-verified cost model I can stress-test myself via sliders (self-service diligence beats narrated claims), a live per-asset QR register that proves operational data systems exist (a core catalytic-capital stewardship question answered without a call), per-number VERIFIED/MODELLED/INTEREST provenance tags (I don't have to hunt for buried caveats), and a concrete ask split with a verified capex line ($110,046) that is IC-memo-ready. (3) The honesty discipline is the strongest single feature: landing the climax on '0 beds assembled in-house — the 50-bed run is the test', showing the cash trough in ALL scenarios, and hard-labelling scabies-to-RHD as reason-not-claimed-outcome makes me trust everything else more — most decks make me discount 30% for hidden caveats; this one pre-discounts itself. (4) Boardroom mode embeds the classic 14-slide discipline inside the same artifact, neutralising the 'immersion wastes my 15 minutes' objection and giving me the printable leave-behind my board actually reads. (5) Thesis + $400K ask stated on screen one — the clarity discipline survives the storytelling. (6) The delta-between-two-trips framing converts $400K from a budget into a mechanism, which is exactly what a program officer needs to champion it internally; memorability matters for internal advocacy between now and 31 Aug. (7) Consent and claim-ceiling risks are identified with specific, credible mitigations rather than hand-waved.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) Marginal value over the counterfactual is smaller than the pitch implies: the genuinely diligence-shortening assets (cost engine, register, LOI ladder) already exist and a classic excellent deck would simply link to them — the immersive wrapper adds memorability and framing, not new evidence. (2) 'A register the founders cannot retroactively edit' is an overstatement — it is their own Supabase and they absolutely can edit it; one detected overclaim makes a sceptic re-audit every other claim, which LENGTHENS diligence. Fix the language to 'timestamped, scan-logged' or it backfires. (3) Opportunity cost: ~3 weeks of a 2-founder team's capacity mid-raise (10 July to ~early Aug) spent on a deck wrapper instead of LOI calls, 50-bed-run prep, and the ~end-July entity/51% decision that I care about far more than any animation. (4) The reverse meter remains emotionally load-bearing for the single most unproven number in the business ($421 modelled); the MODELLED gate helps, but a falling odometer is still animated conviction for a claim with zero in-house production behind it, and a hardened assessor's scepticism reflex will fire precisely at the climax. (5) 13 chapters is long for a time-poor assessor; laptop-first, scroll-linked, video-heavy design degrades on the iPad/boardroom-projector contexts where funders actually open links, and a janky Leaflet+video+odometer moment in a live pitch does damage a static slide never could. (6) The live LOI ladder cuts both ways: if the pipeline is thin on the day I open it, the deck broadcasts a stalling round. (7) The consent re-check for video/audio voices is scheduled late and could gut chapters 5-6 close to the deadline. Net: it raises conviction more than it raises speed, and speed was half the question.

verdicts.strengths: Exceptionally well-grounded — every claimed asset verified to exist in-repo: trip-story.tsx scrollytelling blocks, cost-model-scenarios.ts engine with getMarginalToday(), full /bed/[id] register with scan logging (log-scan.ts), loi-pipeline.ts, all 7 named videos on disk, and existing drift scripts (check-canon-drift.mjs, check-qbe-guardrails.mjs) to extend. All six named voices (Alfred Johnson, Ivy, Karen Liddle, Mykel, Norman Frank, Dianne Stokes) verified present on the cleared-voices.ts external allowlist, whose header explicitly covers funder material; Karen Liddle and Mykel videos already ship publicly on /partners/oonchiumpa. Canon numbers exact: 496/9/16/2,660 match asset-canonical.ts; $685 is canon 'verified' and $421 canon 'modelled' with matching labels. Claim-ceiling discipline exceeds the baseline deck: scabies-to-RHD hard-labelled as reason-not-outcome, 200-350 requests INTEREST-tagged, revenue withheld, the MODELLED click-gate on the reverse meter, and the '0 beds assembled in-house' terminus. Boardroom mode means one artifact serves both the immersive send-ahead and the QBE 15-minute format. Maintenance is genuinely viable for a zero-staff org because every number flows from canonical sources with a proposed import-restriction drift guard — the deck cannot silently rot. The concept pre-names its own four consent/claim pressure points with credible mitigations, which is what a consent-gated org needs.
verdicts.weaknesses: The centerpiece has a verified mechanism misattribution: in cost-model-scenarios.ts, BOTH scenarios include the identical $150 long_haul_freight_per_bed (Buy-Kit $684.79 = 534.79 + 150; Factory $425.74 = 275.74 + 150). The $264 delta is manufacturing-path savings, not road savings — yet the 'freight meter' fuses dollars to distance and implies the reverse ride erases freight cost. The in-deck CostPlayground sliders would let any diligent investor discover freight is constant across both rides, contradicting the climax of a deck whose whole brand is 'trust the register, not the pitch'. Fixable by reframing as a landed-cost meter with an honest delta breakdown, but that dilutes the stated core innovation and must be caught before build, not after. Second: $421 is the canon 'Community' scenario ($0 feedstock + fair-wage assumption, check: manual), not the factory scenario ($425.74) — chapter 7's 'on-country pressing falling to $421' conflates them; assumptions must be surfaced. Third: 'each line item VERIFIED-tagged' in chapter 3 must honor per-line confidence flags, not blanket-tag. Delivery: 3 effort-weeks is the optimistic bound — the scroll-linked meter over video + Leaflet across arbitrary investor laptops, print/PDF boardroom mode, and stranger-proofing a /bed/[id] page built for recipients (recipient_name, contact-row, pulse-check, coop-invite components) realistically lands at 4-5 weeks, crowding the 31 Aug window alongside the entity/51% decision and actual fundraising. The live LOI ladder backfires if the pipeline is thin near deadline (an empty 'round assembling live' is worse than none — needs threshold gating). Video/audio consent re-confirmation is a human bottleneck on Ben in his busiest month; the cut-rule is right but shrinks the deck late. 'A register the founders cannot retroactively edit' overstates — it is their own Supabase; do not let that line into rendered copy.

verdicts.strengths: The core move survives the week-later test with a specific, repeatable sentence: "They made me ride the freight route twice — the cost meter ticked up to a verified $685 on the way out, ran backwards to a modelled $421 on the way home, and the difference was the raise. Then it ended on MY phone, scanning a real bed's live scan log." Two elements are genuinely novel, not repainted: (1) the palindromic structure where the delta between two travelled journeys IS the ask — the investment thesis is experienced as distance-times-dollars rather than narrated, and it is only possible because an invoice-OCR-verified per-component cost engine actually exists in the codebase (verified: cost-model-scenarios.ts, cost-model-context.tsx); (2) the ending that exits the deck entirely onto the investor's own device into live registry infrastructure (/bed/[id] pages verified in-repo) — "trust the register, not the pitch" inverts the epistemics of pitching in a way no AR gimmick or personalized microsite does, because it hands over evidence infrastructure rather than more artifice. The honesty discipline is load-bearing, not decorative: the falling meter physically stopping at a clickable MODELLED gate, and the climax landing on "0 beds assembled in-house — the 50-bed run is the test," converts the biggest claim risk into the deck's most memorable moment. Boardroom mode acknowledges the real QBE 15-minute constraint instead of pretending every investor will scroll for 20 minutes. The concept self-enumerates its consent and claim-ceiling risks with concrete mitigations, which is rare discipline. Feasibility is grounded: every "usesExisting" item was verified present in v2/src.
verdicts.weaknesses: (1) The delivery vehicle is standard fare — scroll-linked map + odometer is well-trodden NYT/Pudding scrollytelling; a skeptical partner could reduce the freight meter to "an animated unit-economics bridge chart." The novelty lives entirely in the palindrome structure and the QR handoff; if either is executed weakly, the rest is a long scrollytelling deck. (2) "A register the founders cannot retroactively edit" is an overclaim — they run the Supabase instance; a diligence-minded investor who notices this will feel the trust framing backfire. It should be softened to "a register you can audit" or backed by append-only/event-log evidence. (3) 13 chapters is long; the climax (reverse meter, chapter 7) and the handoff (chapter 9) sit deep in the scroll, and the boardroom-mode mitigation doubles the rendering/QA surface for a 2-founder team against a hard 31 Aug date. (4) The live QR scan is a single point of failure in the room: boardroom wifi, a slow /bed/[id] cold load, or a stale demo bed kills the best moment — the on-screen fallback link must be rehearsed as the primary path, not the fallback. (5) The persuasive physics of a falling animated number will anchor investors on $421 as fact no matter how many gold MODELLED tags surround it — the concept knows this, but the tension between animation persuasion and honesty discipline is inherent, not fully solvable. (6) Inline CostPlayground sliders mid-narrative risk breaking immersion at the exact emotional peak of the return journey.

concept.name: The Same Road, Twice
concept.tagline: The investor rides the freight route a bed travels today and watches the cost meter climb to a verified $685 — then rides the same road back with the factory On Country and watches it fall to a modelled $421 — and the deck ends not with a slide but with their own phone scanning a real bed in the live register.
concept.coreInnovation: A scroll-linked FREIGHT METER: a Leaflet route animation (city depot to remote community) fused to the invoice-OCR-verified cost model, so distance and dollars accumulate together on screen. Then the deck's pivot move — the identical journey re-run in reverse with on-country manufacture, every falling number wall-to-wall MODELLED-tagged, landing on the honest line "0 beds assembled in-house. The 50-bed run is the test." The investment thesis is not narrated; it is travelled twice, and the delta between the two trips IS the $400K ask. No other deck can do this because no other startup has an invoice-verified per-component cost engine (cost-model-scenarios.ts v6) plus a live QR bed register to hand the investor at the end.
concept.experienceFlow: The investor opens /journey on a laptop, sound optional (captions always on). Black-to-cream prologue in Georgia serif: the one-line thesis and the AU$400K ask stated up front — clarity discipline kept from the baseline deck. Then they start travelling. Chapter 1 puts them on a floor at golden hour with Alfred Johnson's cleared words; the scabies-to-RHD pathway appears explicitly labelled "the reason we exist — not an outcome we claim." Chapter 2 is the first ride: utopia-delivery-road.mp4 under a Leaflet route line, the freight meter ticking components upward until a bought-in bed lands at $685, each line item VERIFIED-tagged from real invoices. They watch a bed assembled in five minutes (assembly.mp4), meet the container factory through the 48-shot process set, then ride the same road back: the meter reverses, $685 falling toward $421, every number gold-tagged MODELLED, ending on the 50-bed-run test their money funds. Canon counters (496 beds, 9 communities, 16 washers, 2,660kg) animate from the live register. Then the handoff: a full-screen QR — they scan it with their own phone and land on a live /bed/[id] page with scan log, event timeline, and map. "Trust the register, not the pitch." Risks show the cash trough honestly; the ask chapter shows the round assembling live from the LOI pipeline; Norman Frank closes. A "boardroom mode" toggle collapses every chapter to its single claim for the 15-minute QBE pitch and prints as the leave-behind.

concept.consentClaimRisks: Four pressure points. (1) The reverse-meter is the deck's climax and its biggest claim risk: $685-to-$421 must be visually saturated with MODELLED tags and must terminate on the '0 beds assembled in-house; the 50-bed run is the test' line — the falling animation may feel like a promise, so the mitigation is that the meter physically stops at a gold 'MODELLED' gate the investor must click through. (2) Video/audio of voices: text quotes were cleared 2026-06-17, but the allowlist must be re-confirmed for moving-image/audio use per speaker (Karen Liddle and Mykel have public-precedent on /partners, which helps but is not consent); ship captions-on, audio opt-in, and cut any voice Ben cannot re-confirm. (3) Route map: animate region-level (reuse utopia/region-map.png geography), never pinpoint homes or un-cleared communities; the scanned demo bed must be one whose owner/community consented to a public register page. (4) Ceiling discipline: scabies-to-RHD appears once, hard-labelled as the why; 200-350 requests always INTEREST; revenue figure withheld pending Standard Ledger sign-off; QBE credit line rendered in the epilogue footer.
concept.whyRemembered: A week later the investor does not remember slides — they remember riding the road twice and watching the same bed cost two different amounts, and they remember that the pitch ended on their own phone, looking at a real bed's live scan log in a register the founders cannot retroactively edit. Every other deck asks to be believed; this one hands over the evidence infrastructure and says check for yourself. That is also exactly the sensation a catalytic funder needs before 31 August: the feeling that the 50-bed run is not a hope but the next tick of a meter they have already watched run.

critique:
**(1) What all six missed**

Two gaps, both structural:

- **The community is a subject, never a presenter.** Every concept stages the investor's experience of verified data; none puts the future owners in the room. The Empathy Ledger has 240 cleared storytellers and the entire raise is an ownership-conversion story — yet no concept has an Elder or community member delivering a segment, no visible community sign-off/consent layer on the deck itself, no "the people you're transferring this to have reviewed this pitch" artifact. A deck about handing a factory to community, pitched entirely without them, is a credibility hole every diligent funder will notice.
- **The second audience: the investment committee.** All six are first-person, experiential, synchronous formats (ride the road, hold the plastic, assemble the term sheet). Real decisions happen when the champion re-pitches to an IC or trust board via a forwarded PDF and a 3-minute skim. None of the concepts degrades gracefully into a forwardable, skimmable, defensible artifact. The deck has no second life after the meeting.

**(2) Strongest combination**

**radical-honesty (The Register, 8.0) + ownership-conversion (The Deal Room, 7.7).** They are complementary halves of one funnel: the Register manufactures trust (clickable evidence, declared unknowns, dated modelled→measured commitments), the Deal Room converts that trust into commitment (assemble your own term sheet against the 31 Aug match deadline). The 30 Sept "come back and diff us" date and the 31 Aug close chain into a single timeline. Note the redundancy elsewhere: living-instrument and wildcard are essentially variants of the Register and add little as standalone concepts — fold their best mechanics (live query, round-assembling-in-real-time final page) into this pairing.

**(3) Cheapest <1-week conviction-raiser**

**A public Claims Register page.** One static Next.js route: every headline number in the raise ($685 freight, $421 modelled, 20kg HDPE/bed, 200kg capacity, unit economics) as a row tagged **measured / modelled / aspiration**, with its source linked (invoice, canon data, cost lab) and a dated commitment for when modelled flips to measured. It requires no new instrumentation — the canon data, cost lab, and gated briefs already exist; this is structured content, not engineering. It is the load-bearing substrate of all six concepts (five of them literally presuppose it), it survives forwarding to a committee (fixing gap #2), and it raises conviction even if the final deck ends up being a plain PDF.
auditDigest:
## AUDIT: deck-current-state
**1) 14-slide structure and claim per slide** (`/home/user/goods-asset-tracker/v2/src/app/deck/page.tsx`, `SLIDES` array; one claim per slide, proof beneath, per Airbnb/LinkedIn/Uber/Buffer/Hoffman patterns cited in the header comment):

1. `cover` — "We press recycled-plastic beds On Country, and build the factory so community owns it." Ask stated up front: AU$400,000 catalytic capital converting into community ownership.
2. `problem` — "Thousands sleep on the floor tonight": freight tax, beds that fail in months, sleep you can't clean.
3. `workaround` — Today's answer is charity drops/freighted goods ending in the dump (~AU$3M/yr Alice Springs washing-machine supplier example).
4. `solution` — Stretch Bed in 3 steps (HDPE X-trestles, 2 steel poles, structural canvas); specs 200kg/26kg/10+yr/20kg plastic. "The magic isn't the bed — the making belongs On Country."
5. `traction` — "Already deployed, already wanted": canon stats from `CANONICAL_ASSETS` (beds deployed, communities, washers, plastic kg — 496/9/16/2,660 per commit 48cd1e3); 200–350 bed requests; Stokes self-fund offer; PICC plant offer; Centrecorp 107-bed deployment.
6. `why-now` — Three shifts: micro-manufacturing tech, Closing the Gap/procurement policy, HDPE + freight economics.
7. `model` — Sell to duty-bound buyers, then in-source plastic: AU$750 price, $685→$426→$421 marginal cost, ~338 beds/yr break-even; 8.6× markup verified on bought-in feedstock.
8. `gtm` — Three wedges with a named buyer each: institutional procurement (Centrecorp), community co-invest (Stokes/PICC), direct + catalytic (live checkout + QBE match).
9. `competition` — 4-column table: freighted furniture / charity drops / do nothing vs "why we win"; "competing with the way beds arrive."
10. `moat` — Compounding advantage: ownership by design (Supply Nation 51% path), cost-down curve, field-proven evidence via Empathy Ledger, Elder-led trust.
11. `team` — Two founders (Marchesi, Knight), 11-member advisory → trigger-gated fiduciary board, OCAP® data sovereignty, 12-month role map.
12. `risks` — 4-row table: cost-down unproven; revenue pending sign-off (hold AU$741,111 until reconciled); entity/51% open (MinterEllison, post-charity ~end July); founder concentration.
13. `ask` — AU$400K recoverable grant (≥1:1 match-eligible): ~$60–80K 50-bed run, ~$200K GM+BD hires, balance press capex (AU$110,046 verified); QBE match live, 31 Aug commitment target, Stage 2 Sept.
14. `thesis` — "Four things to believe" + Norman Frank quote + CTAs to `/impact` and `/cost-story`.

**2) Design system.** Tailwind semantic tokens (`bg-background`, `text-foreground`, `text-primary`, `muted`, `border`, `card`) plus brand CSS vars `--goods-terracotta`, `--goods-gold`, `--goods-clay`, `--goods-teal`. Display type: `var(--font-display, Georgia, serif)`, light weight, up to text-7xl; uppercase tracked eyebrows. Four claim-label pill Tags: **verified** (accent), **modelled** (gold), **interest** (clay), **future** (teal) — used on traction stats, demand box, unit economics, team gaps.

**3) Photos & quotes.** All from `/images/media-pack/` (cleared): `lying-on-stretch-bed.jpg` (cover), `thumbs-up-stretch-bed.jpg` (slide 4), `community-testing-bed-golden-hour.jpg` (slide 5), `nic-with-elder-on-verandah.jpg` (slide 11). Cleared voices (per cleared-voices.ts allowlist, commit 2db7bce): Alfred Johnson, Palm Island (slide 2 freight quote + slide 4 caption), Ivy, Palm Island (slide 5), Dianne Stokes, Tennant Creek (slide 11 caption), Norman Frank, Tennant Creek (slide 14 closing).

**4) Interaction.** Client component, fixed full-screen z-50 chrome-free overlay (`/deck` added to standalone prefixes in `conditional-chrome.tsx`). Keyboard: ArrowRight/Down/Space/PageDown next, ArrowLeft/Up/PageUp prev, Home/End jump. Invisible quarter-width click zones left/right with w/e-resize cursors. Top progress bar, footer dot nav (clickable, desktop-only) + `01 / 14` counter, 0.35s fade-up transition per slide. `layout.tsx`: noindex/nofollow metadata.

**5) Deliberate honesty (claim ceiling).** Baked into copy per header comment: scabies→RHD stated as "the why — not a claimed outcome" (slide 2); demand labelled "interest, not committed revenue"; unit economics labelled "Modelled — 0 beds assembled in-house yet; the 50-bed run is the test"; team tagged "Honest: 0 hires and 0 independent directors yet"; revenue figure withheld pending accountant sign-off; hires and board trigger-gated to milestones; slide 12 frames the whole enterprise as "de-risking-stage." Numbers only from `CANONICAL_ASSETS` (`@/lib/data/asset-canonical`), reconciled 2026-05-30.

## AUDIT: investor-context
**Raw findings — Goods investor deck audit (who it must convince, under what rules)**

**1. Funder archetypes in the live pipeline and what each responds to**
- **QBE Foundation** (corporate foundation, program anchor): responds to climate resilience + inclusion mandate, match-contingent catalytic design, program readiness (PIN session, evidence map, governance docs). Stage 1 $10K committed; up to $400K at Stage 2. Credit line required on all materials: "Catalysing Impact, powered by Social Impact Hub, in partnership with QBE Foundation."
- **Snow Foundation** (family foundation, 2+ yr anchor, ~$493K received per 2026-06-09 Xero reconciliation — supersedes $193K): responds to long-term partnership, fellowship model, co-investment (Jigsaw pattern). 2026 role: R4 grant ($130K target) + a **letter of support as the catalytic signal that opens SEFA/Mindaroo/PFI**.
- **SEFA** (CDFI lender, $9M undeployed): responds to predictable revenue by a known date ("debt brings cash flow from the future into the now"), institutional B2B contracts, and its **governance covenant: majority independent directors** (or structured advisory group). Ask: $300K working-capital sub-debt; possible syndicate agent (Jigsaw template). Stage: cold outreach (email Hannah Ebeling).
- **PFI (Paul Ramsay)** (private foundation): recoverable/repayable grant, $640K target, EOI in their DD.
- **Minderoo/Mindaroo** (catalytic bucket): recoverable grant, $200K target, relationship-only; geographic match unconfirmed.
- **IBA** (government Indigenous fund): debt+grant blend up to $5M; needs a business plan; responds to Indigenous ownership pathway.
- **First Australians Capital** (Indigenous CDFI): $100–500K debt, research stage.
- **Tim Fairfax** (family foundation): $50–150K grant, advisory stage.
- **QBE Ventures** (CVC equity): deferred to 2027 — fails knockouts.

**2. Knockout criteria (any single fail = drop)** — CASE six filters: (1) geography — must invest in remote NT/QLD/WA/SA; (2) legal structure — CLG + Pty Ltd hybrid; (3) size — $100K–$5M tranche-dependent; (4) return expectations — 5–10yr, social-first, community-ownership exit (no conventional exit); (5) capital type — grant/recoverable/sub-debt/senior debt (not equity in 2026); (6) stage — early commercial, post-prototype pre-scale. Currently pass all six: QBE Foundation, SEFA, Snow, IBA, PFI, Mindaroo, FAC. Reverse knockouts on Goods' side: SEFA's independent-director covenant (0 independent directors today); do NOT claim the $2.5–5M raise is modelled, the ownership vehicle decided, impact metrics live, resident purchase proven, or debt serviceable without a model.

**3. The exact ask and mechanism** — AU$400,000 in signed, **match-eligible catalytic capital**: a **recoverable grant that converts into community ownership**. Match rules: at least 1:1, repayable preferred; QBE Stage 2 provides up to $400K from a $1M cohort pool contingent on matched external investment. Use of funds: 50-bed in-source production run (AU$60–80K, the proof gate turning the cost claim from modelled to measured), GM + BD hires (~AU$200K), plant/facility capex. Two contract clauses to clear pre-signing: cl 7.3 (IP) and cl 5.3 (SIH co-invest).

**4. Hard deadlines** — First signed match-eligible commitment: **31 August 2026**. QBE **Stage 2 application: September 2026** (pitch + financial model + LOIs + governance docs); Steering Committee October; funding outcomes November 2026.

**5. Honesty gates for every external artifact**
- **Claim ceiling:** scabies→RHD pathway is the *why*, never a claimed outcome; no prevented-disease or savings claims; no health/justice outcomes claimed.
- **Consent allowlist:** only cleared-for-external community voices.
- **Canon numbers only** (`v2/src/lib/data/asset-canonical.ts`): 496 beds (133 Stretch, 363 Basket), 9 communities, 16 washers, 2,660kg at 20kg/bed (modelled). Revenue: hold AU$741,111 publicly until accountant signs one Goods-only figure (~AU$907,569).
- **Label everything verified / modelled / future**; demand is interest not committed revenue; 0 beds assembled in-house — cost-down is a hypothesis.
